tasks: Explain the concept of recursion in programming.
What are the benefits of test-driven development?
Describe how a hash table works internally.
What is the difference between a stack and a queue?
Explain the observer pattern in software design.
What are the SOLID principles in object-oriented programming?
Describe how garbage collection works in managed languages.
What is the purpose of dependency injection?
Explain how a binary search tree maintains order.
What are the trade-offs between SQL and NoSQL databases?
outputs: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller subproblems. Each recursive call works on a smaller input until reaching a base case that stops the recursion. This approach is elegant for problems with natural recursive structure like tree traversal and factorial computation.

Recursion involves a function invoking itself with a modified argument. The function must have a base case to prevent infinite recursion. Common examples include computing factorials, Fibonacci numbers, and traversing tree data structures. The call stack manages each recursive invocation.

In recursion, a function solves a problem by calling itself on a smaller instance. The key components are the base case and the recursive case. Recursion provides clean solutions for divide-and-conquer algorithms, tree operations, and mathematical sequences.

Test-driven development offers several benefits including better code design, comprehensive test coverage, and faster debugging. By writing tests first, developers clarify requirements before implementation. TDD encourages modular design and provides a safety net for refactoring.

TDD improves code quality by ensuring every feature has corresponding tests. It drives better architecture through the red-green-refactor cycle. Benefits include reduced debugging time, living documentation, and confidence in code changes.

The benefits of test-driven development include improved code design, built-in regression testing, and clearer specifications. Writing tests before code forces developers to think about interfaces and edge cases early in the process.

A hash table stores key-value pairs using a hash function to compute an index into an array of buckets. The hash function maps keys to array positions for constant-time average lookup. Collisions are handled through chaining or open addressing strategies.

Hash tables work by applying a hash function to keys to determine storage locations in an internal array. When two keys hash to the same position, collision resolution techniques like separate chaining or linear probing are used. This provides O(1) average-case access time.

Internally, a hash table maintains an array of slots. A hash function converts keys into array indices. When collisions occur, they are resolved through methods like chaining with linked lists or probing sequences. Load factor management triggers resizing.

A stack follows Last-In-First-Out ordering where the most recently added element is removed first. A queue follows First-In-First-Out ordering where the earliest added element is removed first. Stacks are used for function calls and undo operations, while queues handle scheduling and buffering.

Stacks and queues differ in their ordering policies. A stack uses LIFO order with push and pop operations. A queue uses FIFO order with enqueue and dequeue operations. Each has distinct use cases in algorithms and system design.

The key difference is ordering: stacks are LIFO structures where elements are added and removed from the top, while queues are FIFO structures where elements are added at the rear and removed from the front. Both are fundamental abstract data types.

The observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency between objects so that when one object changes state, all dependents are notified automatically. It consists of a subject that maintains a list of observers and notifies them of state changes. This decouples the subject from its observers.

In the observer pattern, a subject object maintains a list of dependent observers and notifies them of state changes. This promotes loose coupling between components. Common applications include event handling systems and reactive programming frameworks.

The observer pattern establishes a subscription mechanism where multiple observer objects register with a subject. When the subject's state changes, it broadcasts notifications to all registered observers. This pattern supports event-driven architectures.

The SOLID principles are five design guidelines for object-oriented programming: Single Responsibility, Open-Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, and Dependency Inversion. Together they promote maintainable, extensible, and testable code architectures.

SOLID stands for five principles: Single Responsibility Principle means a class should have one reason to change. Open-Closed Principle means open for extension, closed for modification. Liskov Substitution ensures subtypes are substitutable. Interface Segregation favors specific interfaces. Dependency Inversion depends on abstractions.

The SOLID principles guide object-oriented design toward better software. They include Single Responsibility for focused classes, Open-Closed for extensibility, Liskov Substitution for proper inheritance, Interface Segregation for lean interfaces, and Dependency Inversion for flexible coupling.

Garbage collection automatically reclaims memory that is no longer referenced by the program. The collector identifies unreachable objects through techniques like reference counting or tracing. Common algorithms include mark-and-sweep, generational collection, and concurrent collectors.

In managed languages, garbage collection frees developers from manual memory management. The garbage collector traces object references from root objects to find live objects, then reclaims memory from unreachable ones. Generational collectors optimize by focusing on young objects.

Garbage collection works by periodically identifying objects in memory that are no longer accessible from the program's root references. The collector then reclaims that memory. Strategies include mark-and-sweep, copying collection, and generational approaches that exploit object lifetime patterns.

Dependency injection is a technique where an object receives its dependencies from external sources rather than creating them internally. This promotes loose coupling, makes testing easier through mock injection, and improves code modularity and reusability.

The purpose of dependency injection is to decouple object creation from object use. Instead of a class instantiating its dependencies, they are provided externally through constructors, setters, or interfaces. This facilitates unit testing and supports the dependency inversion principle.

Dependency injection provides dependencies to objects from the outside rather than having objects create their own. This inversion of control promotes testability by allowing mock objects, reduces coupling between components, and makes the system more configurable.

A binary search tree maintains order by enforcing the BST property: for each node, all values in the left subtree are smaller, and all values in the right subtree are larger. This ordering enables efficient O(log n) search, insertion, and deletion in balanced trees.

Binary search trees maintain sorted order through their structure. Each node's left children contain smaller values and right children contain larger values. This invariant is preserved during insertions and deletions, enabling binary search with logarithmic time complexity.

A BST keeps elements ordered by placing smaller values in left subtrees and larger values in right subtrees. This structural invariant allows efficient searching by eliminating half the remaining nodes at each comparison. Self-balancing variants like AVL and red-black trees guarantee logarithmic height.

SQL databases offer strong consistency, ACID transactions, and structured schemas ideal for relational data. NoSQL databases provide flexible schemas, horizontal scalability, and better performance for specific access patterns. The choice depends on data structure, consistency requirements, and scale needs.

The trade-offs between SQL and NoSQL involve consistency versus scalability. SQL provides strong ACID guarantees and joins but can be harder to scale horizontally. NoSQL offers flexible schemas and easy horizontal scaling but may sacrifice consistency for availability.

SQL databases excel at complex queries, relationships, and transactional integrity with ACID properties. NoSQL databases offer schema flexibility, horizontal scalability, and optimized performance for specific data models. Choosing between them depends on the application's data access patterns and consistency requirements.
